name: Rod — framework-native AI chief-of-staff
next_move: Build the quadtree-version of Rod Jake mentioned at end of raw transcript — 'the quadtree mechanism could be way, way more real at learning how to do this over time better.' Likely lands alongside the seven-type-comparison work (Open Questions 5.3).
blockers: Quadtree subdivision trigger conditions still open (Open Questions 5.3)
Grade+note as feedback standard not yet promoted to design doc (Open Questions 9.10)
live_band: Three views (Briefing / Proposals / Drafts), two-mouse demo mechanic (human clicks blue bubbles, Rod operates DOM). Explicit division of labor: human = regime-setter + commit-signer + teacher; agent = anticipation-field-assembler + draft-producer + DOM-executor. Every proposal routes through the fourfold exit map (Sign it A / Grade B with refinement / — too thin / Wait). Grade+note teaches Rod which sore-thumb variables carry which weights. 'Why it sounds like you' panel surfaces the sediment Rod has accumulated.
notes: Jake's biggest idea for what semi-autonomous AI collaboration can look like — 'feels EXCELLENT to use in practice.' Sibling product to Secretary: both framework-native, both enforce typed abstention, both route decisions through the fourfold exit. Rod's design choices (grade+note, four-option response pattern, two-mouse visibility) are candidates for framework-native AI product design standards. The asymmetric-advantage claim: users of this interface operate with visible anchor/comparator discipline that's structurally unavailable to users of conventional AI assistants.
